steps.title: Find the axis (pencil)
steps.description: Make sure you're in pencil mode. Lightly sketch a tall vertical line for the shank — the central shaft of the anchor. This axis is the spine of the whole drawing.

steps.title: Mark the ring and crown (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, sketch a small circle at the top of the shank for the ring, and a short horizontal line near the top for the stock — the crossbar. Mark a dot at the bottom of the shank where the arms will branch.

steps.title: Block the arms and flukes (pencil)
steps.description: From the bottom dot, sketch two curved lines arcing outward and upward, one to each side, like a wide smile. At the end of each arm, sketch a small triangular fluke — the pointed paddle shape that bites the seabed.

steps.title: Draw through the back (pencil)
steps.description: Lightly sketch the back edge of the curved arms even where they'll sit behind the shank. Drawing the full form now keeps the anchor feeling solid in three dimensions.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the shank and ring
steps.description: Switch to ink. Trace the shank as one confident vertical stroke, the ring as a single circle, and the stock as a clean horizontal bar. Where the stock crosses the shank, let the lines meet at the junction cleanly.

steps.title: Ink the arms and flukes (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, trace each arm in one curving pass from the bottom of the shank out to its fluke. Outline each fluke as a small triangle. Stop the back arm's line where the shank crosses in front — don't draw through.

steps.title: Clean up
steps.description: Switch to the eraser and remove the construction lines, including the back edges you drew through. The anchor should read as one connected piece of iron.
